data.title: Oil Up, S&P 500 Up
data.description:
Since the conflict in the Middle East began, investors have been using a very simple heuristic for telling whether the S&P 500 would be up or down.

One only had to look at oil prices to gauge, directionally, what the S&P 500 was doing on a certain day. That trend (oil up, equities down) broke this week. On April 22, to everyone's surprise, despite the war headlines and the jump in crude prices, both the technology and energy sectors were in the green.

By the end of April 22, the State Street Technology Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLK) was up 2.2%, and the State Street Energy Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLE) was up 1.2%.

When stocks rise on negative headlines, the market may be seeing something the rest of us have not caught yet.

In this piece, I discuss what that may be. I will also provide an overview of the recent events in the Middle East, which are anything but encouraging. Therefore, the near term may remain volatile.

As for me, I remain fully invested in U.S. equities as I prefer to look beyond the near-term headlines.

Looking Beyond The War Headlines

As I said in many previous articles, it's incredibly easy to get caught up in the tsunami of new headlines coming from the Middle East, similar to what happened last year with the tariffs.

I will review the latest developments on the conflict in the next section. Here, I'm mainly trying to make sense of the reasons why (finally), the S&P 500 wasn't down when the energy sector (XLE) was up.

As seen above, the inverse relationship between the two was blatantly obvious until April 22. That day, the XLE was up 1.2%, and the SP500 was up 0.59%.

The heuristic that we've all been using since the start of the war was very simple: if oil is up, energy is up, and U.S. equities are down.

Seeing the first signs of a decoupling in that relationship is very encouraging. Here are my arguments as to why the market is looking past the war headlines.

First, consumer spending reaccelerated in Q1, with March advance retail and food services sales up 1.7% m/m and 4.0% y/y, despite the war headlines.

In fact, the January-February retail sales were revised up to a combined 0.7% increase before March’s gain, which means the March print was an acceleration on top of a firmer starting point than previously reported.

The data says the consumer spending remains healthy. Within March retail sales, non-store retailers were up 10.1% y/y, and food services and drinking places were up 2.4% y/y.

That spending power is flowing into corporate earnings. In fact, early Q1 earnings show earnings growing about 3.3 percentage points faster than revenue. So far, the Q1 2026 earnings look particularly encouraging, according to the FactSet Research Systemns Inc. (DS) April 17 update:

Overall, 10% of the companies in the S&P 500 have reported actual results for Q1 2026 to date. Of these companies, 88% have reported actual EPS above estimates, which is above the 5-year average of 78% and above the 10-year average of 76%.

Here are two "fun facts" from FactSet's latest earnings season update:

If 9.9% is the actual revenue growth rate for the quarter, it will mark the highest revenue growth rate reported by the index since Q3 2022 (11.0%).

If 13.2% is the actual growth rate for the quarter, it will mark the 6th consecutive quarter of double-digit (year-over-year) earnings growth for the index.

It's my view that the earnings story will prevail over the war headlines. That's why, in my view, we've seen energy and the S&P 500 up on April 22. Even if the negative headlines from the Middle East don't take a pause, I expect this trend to persist as more companies report Q1 earnings.

By the way, analysts are now expecting 18% earnings growth for CY 2026, with most of the upside happening in the second half of the year. That's why I feel more bullish in H2 than in H1.

For reference, here are the latest analyst estimates for each quarter:

  • Q1 2026: 13.2%
  • Q2 2026: 20.1%
  • Q3 2026: 22.2%
  • Q4 2026: 19.9%

I will conclude this section with the chart below, which I strongly believe should not be ignored by the bears on the broader markets.

As seen above, since the start of the war, the bottom-up EPS estimates for both 2026 and 2027 have been revised upwards. That should tell you the resilience of the U.S. economy despite the negative headlines coming from the Middle East (and the implications on gas prices).

Is The Ceasefire Still On?

In an epic and quite expected move, Trump backed off (again) from his threats to renew attacks on Iran’s energy infrastructure, extending the ceasefire until Iran submits a proposal and discussions are concluded.

Interestingly, it appears that Iran did not formally agree to that extension. In other words, it seems to me that the extension of the ceasefire has been a unilateral decision.

From what I'm reading, Iran parliament speaker and lead negotiator Mohammad Baqer Qalibaf said a full ceasefire only made sense if the U.S. blockade of Iran’s maritime trade were lifted.

As I wrote a while ago, the U.S. blockade of Iran-bound vessels is pretty much the main leverage that the U.S. has in this war. Therefore, I don’t think the U.S. will reopen free passage to Iran unless a deal is made. That's exactly why I've seen the U.S. blockade as the first major step toward the end of the conflict.

That didn't happen yet.

Since the U.S. blockade, it seems that over 30 vessels have been directed by the U.S. Navy to turn around or return to port. In fact, on April 22, the U.S. intercepted three Iranian-flagged vessels: the Deep Sea, Sevin, and Dorena. Interestingly, it seems that the Deep Sea was part loaded, the Sevin was carrying about 65% of its 1 million barrel capacity, and Dorena was fully loaded with 2 million barrels of crude.

In fact, there are some reports that suggest that a fourth tanker, Derya, may also have been intercepted after failing to discharge its cargo in India

Counterintuitively, I believe these recent interceptions are great news for ending the conflict, as it gives the U.S. more leverage for the next round of talks.

That said, Iran is also gaining leverage after the IRGC seized the Liberia-flagged Epaminondas and Panama-flagged MSC Francesca. As White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt suggested, these seizures are piracy acts, but they are not a ceasefire violation because the ships were not U.S. or Israeli. In fact, it appears that a third Liberia-flagged container ship was fired upon in the same area but was not damaged and later resumed sailing.

In other words, the leverage that Iran won with these seizures may be limited.

So far, the actions from both sides are still directed at commercial vessels. The risk here is that either party decides to escalate, targeting military ships. Given the cadence of the recent vessel seizures, I think the market is growing increasingly concerned about this scenario.

As seen above, the major US benchmarks were down today in the premarket session, with the SPY almost flat in the last week.

Looking Ahead

In the immediate near term, I still see risks from the conflict in the Middle East. That said, even though my timeframe is significantly shorter than that of investors like Warren Buffett, over a 3 to 9-month period, I still see the S&P 500 hitting new all-time highs.

As I've shown in this piece, the earnings growth story remains intact. In fact, EPS estimates for 2026 and 2027 were revised to the upside since the start of the war.

Therefore, the chart below showing Brent oil prices nearing $100 is not making me sweat as much as back in early March this year.

Whatever happens in the near term is impossible to predict, as no one knows (at least, that I want to believe) what Trump's next move will be. Certainly, the recent developments in the Middle East are not particularly encouraging, especially given the fact that the next round of talks is not scheduled.

Overall, I prefer to look at the signal and not at the noise. I say "noise" because the impact of higher gas prices is not spilling over (meaningfully) to core CPI.

Therefore, I believe the strong Q1 earnings season, coupled with what I believe may be a shift in the market’s excessive pessimism on rate cuts, will lift the broader markets later this year.

data.title: KBWD: Private Credit Weighs Amid Broader Financials-Sector Relative Weakness (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
The Financials sector has quietly lagged the S&P 500 by a very wide margin in the past 12 months. The gap is on the order of 24%, and few areas within the once-leading sector have been unscathed versus the SPX. Among the laggards is the high-yield banking niche.

The Invesco KBW High Dividend Yield Financial ETF (KBWD) owns a mix of regional banks, BDCs, REITs, and other high-yield (often low-quality) Financials sector plays. I had a hold rating on the ETF back in Q3 of 2025. Shares are down 6% since then, underperforming the S&P 500 by almost 14%.

Despite some signs of life returning to the high-yield factor, KBWD has not climbed out of its years-long downtrend. The valuation is very compelling, but this is one of those situations where price action must at least partly confirm the valuation story. I reiterate a hold today.

KBWD, XLF Lagging the S&P 500 YoY

High-Yield Factor Up YTD

High-Yield Factor Solid Last 6 Months

According to the issuer, KBWD invests in companies principally engaged in the business of providing financial services and products, as determined by the Index provider. The Fund and the Index are rebalanced and reconstituted quarterly. KBWD generally will invest at least 90% of its total assets in the securities of publicly listed financial companies with competitive dividend yields.

KBWD is a small ETF, with just $440 million in assets under management as of April 22, 2026. That’s up modestly from $397 million at the time of my previous analysis. The annual expense ratio is very high at 5.4%, while the trailing 12-month dividend yield is very high at 13.4%. Keep in mind that KBWD owns several closed-end funds (CEFs), which employ leverage, pushing up the stated expense ratio.

Share-price momentum has been lackluster lately, currently earning the product a soft C- ETF Grade in that category by Seeking Alpha’s quantitative scoring system. Along with negative alpha to the broader market, KBWD’s risk metrics are not encouraging. Realized volatility is low, but the ETF has high turnover. That, along with the big yield, can cause a tax drag. Thus, I’d encourage prospective investors to hold the product in a tax-sheltered account versus a taxable account.

Finally, on liquidity, there are mixed marks. Average daily volume is a bit above 350,000 shares, while the median 30-day bid/ask spread is a touch wide at 8 basis points.

For an update on the portfolio, the 1-star, negatively rated ETF by Morningstar plots almost exclusively in the lower-left corner of the style box, indicating its high weight to US small-cap value. Thus, much hinges on the macroeconomic outlook, namely the direction of interest rates (higher rates could hurt the yield-sensitive plays) and GDP growth (improved growth is generally a boon to its cyclical positions).

On valuation, the price-to-earnings ratio is no doubt compelling, at barely more than 7x. Pair that with a long-term EPS growth rate of 12%, and the resulting PEG ratio is attractive. Of course, KBWD has been cheap on paper for quite a while, and we’ve yet to see a price-action spark to send the fund rallying more than the Financials sector or S&P 500.

KBWD: Portfolio & Factor Profiles

Zooming in on the sector breakdown, the 100% Financials-sector ETF holds just 43 positions. No single name is more than 4.31%. You’ll notice that many BDCs and CEFs command a large share of the product.

I urge investors not to fall in love with the yield, as many of these types of holdings are quite risky on their own, particularly as private credit fears continue to permeate the market.

KBWD: Holdings & Dividend Information

Seasonally, there’s likewise not a whole lot to get excited about. The April through June stretch has featured mixed price returns in the past 10 years, while some mid-year strength has been apparent.

Keep in mind that the below view is for prices only (not dividends).

KBWD: Uninspiring Calendar Trends

The Technical Take

With a very low valuation, high yield, and Financials-sector focus, KBWD’s technical situation is not strong. Notice in the chart below that the ETF now has a defined downtrend resistance line with which to battle. This comes after a solid rise off the April 2025 bottom, which then paired with a comparable low last month. Hence, there is a bullish double-bottom feature that helps to offset the bearish downtrend resistance line.

But take a look at the long-term 200-day moving average. It’s on the decline, suggesting that the bears control the primary trend. Moreover, there is a high amount of volume by price up to the mid-teens, which will make rally attempts tough on the longs. Having tagged technical oversold conditions at the March low, we could see a further bounce, but $14.40 is where the bears may look to tighten their grip once more.

KBWD: Downtrend Established, Double-Bottom Encouraging

The Bottom Line

I have a hold rating on KBWD. The high-yield Financials sector play, housing income-producing stocks, BDCs, REITs, and CEFs, remains mired in a downtrend, while producing underperforming total returns. Macro conditions continue to weigh.

data.title: Archrock: A Buy Driven By Higher Growth Projections And Natural Gas Demand Surge
data.description:
I covered natural gas compression provider, Archrock Inc. (AROC) last June, and since then the stock has risen 41.64% and is up 54.20% (YoY). The company released its Q4 2025 and FY 2025 earnings, with annual revenues increasing 28.7% (YoY) to a record ~$1.5 billion, while adjusted net income hit a record $332.7 million, representing an increase of 79.69% (YoY). In this analysis, I will discuss why I am maintaining my hold rating on this stock, despite its reduction in adjusted EBITDA guidance for FY 2026 compared to FY 2025, given that it hit record earnings in FY 2025. I still believe the expanding demand for natural gas in the US and beyond presents a good opportunity for the company to grow its revenue and profitability. AROC’s management is also optimistic about its operational growth while targeting to lower its leverage ratio to under 3X, which will also be evaluated in this analysis.

AROC recorded a net income of $117 million in Q4 2025 (+64.04%, QoQ) alongside an adjusted EBITDA of $269 million. Growth metrics suggest that AROC’s forward annual revenue for the FY 2026 may rise by at least 12.44% (YoY), meaning that it could reach $1.7 billion from the $1.5 billion recorded in the FY 2025.

AROC also hit a historic adjusted net income of $332.7 million (+79.63%, YoY) and a record adjusted EBITDA of $901 million. However, the company’s annual guidance for the FY 2026 fell to the range of $865 million and $915 million (a midpoint of $890 million). It represented a decline of $11 million or 1.24% (YoY). Despite this apparent decline, the consideration of the “$23 million cash benefit related to cost of sales of contract operations” and the “$32 million net gains from compression sales” means that the actual adjusted EBITDA for the FY 2025 (midpoint) was $846 million, i.e., $901 million less $55 million. Essentially, the adjusted guidance of $890 million presents a projected growth of 5.2% (YoY) for the FY 2026.

Then again, AROC’s FY 2026 CapEx guidance sits at $400 million to $445 million (a midpoint of $422.5 million). This CapEx is already higher than the adjusted net income (denoting that AROC’s management prefers to expand its capacity and growth prospects, which partly contributes to its indebtedness.

As of December 31, 2025, AROCK’s outstanding debt balance stood at $2.4 billion (due in 2028 and 2032, according to its 10-K), against an adjusted EBITDA of $901 million (in 2025). It presents a leverage ratio of 2.7X. In FY 2026, the leverage ratio is still expected to be 2.7X (a net debt of $2.4 billion against an EBITDA of $890 million), showing that AROC is still capable of paying down its debt within the periods of 2028 and 2032, even if it did not use all its operating cash flow to do so. I believe AROC’s goal is to keep growing its adjusted EBITDA since it stated in its Q4 2025 earnings report that it intended to keep its leverage ratio below 3X (in the near time). It has reduced this leverage ratio from 3.3X as of the FY 2024 to 2.7X in FY 2025 (a 22.22%, YoY reduction), showing its commitment to pay down debt into 2026.

Further thoughts on CapEx and expected Demand growth in 2026 and beyond

The CapEx (according to the Q4 2025 earnings call) is divided into growth ($250 million to $275 million), maintenance ($125 million to $135 million and other CapEx ($25 million to $35 million). Of this amount, AROC is set to spend the growth CapEx ($250 million to $275 million, a midpoint of $262.5 million) to add 170,000 horsepower for the natural gas compression in the FY 2026. It is vital to note that AROC’s total horsepower in operation in the FY 2025 stood at 4.6 million. This addition means that AROC’s total horsepower in the FY 2026 is likely to be 4.77 million. With 60% expected to be delivered in H1 2026 (i.e., 2.862 million horsepower), we are expected to see the remaining 1.908 million horsepower delivered in H2 2026.

An aspect that could warrant an increase in horsepower above the guided projection is demand growth. As of this writing, the Iranian war was still ongoing, with some ramifications being a naval blockade affecting millions of barrels of oil per day and the disruption of the supply of natural gas. Apart from the unsettling nature currently in the Middle East, a key global oil and gas hub, and the closure of the Strait of Hormuz, it also led to the damage of two key Qatar LNG trains owned by QatarEnergy. Reports show that the repairs to the damage will affect the production and supply of “12.8 million tons of LNG for 3 to 5 years.” The damage has affected about 17% of QatarEnergy’s export capacity, resulting in an annual revenue loss of at least $20 billion. Such a situation has created a gap essential for US LNG companies to fill. Keeping in mind that LNG is a central driver for the heightened demand for natural gas with related infrastructure, including compression, which is readily provided by, among other companies, AROC.

Analysts anticipate that in 2026, LNG prices will reach a record 17 Bcf/day.

Reports from the EIA support this claim, as shown in the following quote,

We estimate U.S. LNG exports were 17.9 Bcf/d in March, an 8% increase over our January STEO forecast and the second-highest LNG export volume on record following December 2025. The widening spread between domestic and international prices as a result of continued disruptions to LNG exports through the Strait of Hormuz encourages increased LNG exports from the United States, although capacity is constrained. We forecast that full-year 2026 LNG exports will total 17.0 Bcf/d, up from 16.4 Bcf/d in our January forecast, and 2027 exports will total 18.6 Bcf/d, up 0.5 Bcf/d from our January forecast, both above the previous annual record of 15.1 Bcf/d set in 2025.”

Valuation

I will use the enterprise value/Sales (EV/Sales) ratio to evaluate AROC as opposed to the PEG ratio that I used in my previous article on AROC, since it has a high revenue growth rate, at 28.7% (YoY) and a projected +12.44% (YoY) in the FY 2026, relative to net income. This multiple will also avoid low earnings since AROC’s topline numbers into 2026 (as seen in this analysis) are gaining traction as it is expanding its market base. This metric will also take into consideration AROC's debt level, as it is capital-intensive with a leverage ratio of 2.7X (projected for 2026) from 3.3X in 2024.

AROC’s forward EV/Sales ratio stands at 5.48X. This analysis has shown that AROC’s forward revenue sits at $1.7 billion (against ~$1.5 billion in the FY 2025). To calculate the EV, I will multiply the forward EV/Sales multiple (5.48X) by the forward sales value of $1.7 billion, which equals to $9.316 billion. The company’s cash balance as of 2026 stood at $1.55 million (which we will add to the EV). We will then subtract the debt balance of $2.4 billion (from the total of EV+ cash) to get the market capitalization that equals to $6.92 billion. To get the future price, I will divide the market cap ($6.92 billion) by the total outstanding share count that stood at 174.5 million, as of February 2026, which gives us $39.66. As of this writing, AROC’s share price stood at $35.64, meaning that the stock has been undervalued by 11.3% (YoY).

I will compare the performance of AROC with some of its peers.

Fwd. EV/Sales Fwd. Sales Fwd. EV Fwd. Market Cap Future Share Price Current Share Price Valuation Difference Dividend Yield
AROC 5.48X $1.7 billion $9.316 billion $6.92 billion $39.66 $35.64 +11.3% (YoY) undervalued 2.50%
Kodiak Gas Services, Inc. (KGS) 5.44X $1.47 billion $8 billion $8.6 billion $99.98 $63.70 +56.95% (YoY) undervalued 3.11%
USA Compression Partners, LP Common Units. (USAC) 4.90X $1.131 billion $5.542 billion $3.011 $20.76 $26.83 -29.24% (YoY) overvalued 7.94%

This tabulated analysis shows that KGS is the most undervalued with a possible upside of about 57% (YoY), followed by AROC with a projected upside of 11% (YoY). USAC appears to be overvalued, with a projected downside of 29.24% (YoY). However, USAC has the highest dividend yield of the 3 at 7.94%, followed by KGS at 3.11% and finally AROC at 2.50%.

Further, AROC raised its guidance for the cash available for dividend to the range of $572 million to $602 million (a midpoint of $587 million) from $364.6 million, representing an increase of almost 61% (YoY).

Risk

I have based my valuation on AROC’s attainment of its 12.44% (YoY) revenue growth projection for the FY 2026. Failure to attain this revenue growth will harm the valuation and lower the prediction made herein.

The difference between AROC’s net income and its overall revenue is 347.79%, indicating that costs and expenses have absorbed almost 3.5 times the company’s revenue. Thereby, an increase in Archrock's debt portfolio or its failure to pay down debt in 2026 will affect this valuation and negatively impact the share price.

Thoughts on upcoming earnings

AROC is expected to release its Q1 2026 earnings in early May 2026. I anticipate contract operations to record higher sales than Q1 2025 since it has already contracted 85% of its backlog. I also expect an update on the operational growth, including the management's call on maintaining the leverage ratio at 2.7X in 2026.

Bottom Line

I have maintained my buy rating for Archrock with my valuation indicating a possible upside of 11% (YoY). My analysis also shows that the adjusted EBITDA guidance of $890 million for the FY 2026 represents a potential upside of about 5.2% (YoY). I have also established that the company is able to pay down its debt between 2028 and 2032 with a leverage ratio of 2.7X (of its net debt against adjusted EBITDA). Overall, AROC is set to benefit from the rising LNG prices expected in 2026, including the rising demand for natural gas and related infrastructure globally. I await the next earnings date to update my analysis.

data.title: Global Energy Shock Sends Stock Markets On Divergent Paths
data.description:
The Iran conflict has triggered “the biggest energy security threat in history,” according to Fatih Birol, head of the International Energy Agency (IEA), speaking on CNBC yesterday. Yet, the impact will not be felt uniformly, a disparity that likely plays a role in the varied responses in global stock markets to date.

Using a set of ETFs (and one closed-end fund for Central and Eastern Europe) to track investor sentiment on a regional basis highlights a wide mix of performances since the war started on Feb. 28 through yesterday’s close (Apr. 22). Initially, stocks fell just about everywhere, but in late March, a rebound kicked in, although the recovery has been conspicuously uneven, driven in part by differences in energy vulnerability related to the sharp drop in oil exports from the Gulf region.

Leading the winners: stocks in Central and Eastern Europe (CEE) are up 5%. That contrasts with a 7.8% loss for equities in Africa (AFK).

US shares (SPY) are in the winner’s circle via a 4% gain since hostilities started.

Notably, a globally diversified portfolio of stocks (VT) has recovered and is currently up 1.8%. A key driver of that gain comes from US shares: a global equities fund ex-US (VXUS) is still down 1.4% since Feb. 28.

The question is how markets will price in the energy risk that Fatih Birol outlined. The pain will vary dramatically, depending on the level of reliance on energy imports. Asia is especially vulnerable.

“The war in the Middle East and the ensuing energy supply shock are raising inflation, weakening external balances, and narrowing policy options, underscoring the region’s dependence on imported oil and gas,” the IMF advised. "Even so, we project Asia to remain the main driver of global growth." The 5 percent expansion last year will moderate to 4.4% and 4.2% this year and next, according to the reference forecast in the latest World Economic Outlook that assumes the energy shock proves transient. We expect China and India to contribute 70% of the region’s growth.

Nonetheless, “The headwinds will test Asia’s resilience,” the IMF continued.

The same will be true for the rest of the world, although as varying results in stock markets suggest, the effects will be distributed asymmetrically.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Alexandria Q1 Preview: Expecting Positive Progress On Reset, 'Strong Buy' Long-Term
data.description:
Alexandria Real Estate (ARE) is undergoing a strategic reset, one that investors appear to have no interest in waiting out.

I believe sentiment will ultimately turn for the better over the long term, and I believe those that stick it out could be greatly rewarded with outsized market returns.

Shares in ARE have lost 75% of their value over the last five years. This includes a nearly 40% decline over the past year.

This coincides with the ongoing bear market in biotech, one of ARE’s largest sector exposures. Since 2021, availability of life science real estate has grown 7.5x. That’s proving to be a crushing supply-side statistic for ARE.

ARE is bearing the bear by doing what it has always done best. That is, maintaining its strong balance sheet and significantly adjusting capital needs.

For 2026, ARE intends to continue reducing the size of its existing asset base, while focusing on substantially growing its megacampuses.

Investors are skeptical and see ARE in a very much ‘show me’ mode. While sentiment among the Seeking Alpha (“SA”) community is bullish overall, much of the recent coverage has been at the ‘hold’ end of the spectrum.

For my part, I am strongly bullish, and I believe better days await ARE. This certainly is not going to happen overnight, and it's likely not going to happen following ARE’s Q1 release, which is set for the post-market hours of Monday, the 27th. But I ultimately believe ARE will overcome its challenges, and I can see its shares rebounding back to the $90/share level over the medium to long term.

ARE Stock Key Metrics

Stripping out the biotech noise and just looking at ARE as a REIT, I believe that ARE still looks like one of the highest-quality real estate platforms in the public markets.

The portfolio ended 2025 at 90.9% occupancy, up sequentially, with +1.2M square feet (“SF”) of leasing in Q4 alone. That’s not bad for a bear market. Not to mention that this was also the strongest quarter of the year.

In addition, nearly 900K SF of signed leases are set to commence in 2H26. This represents +$52M of incremental annual rent. Not small change by any means.

Furthermore, Megacampuses account for 78% of annual rental revenue and are outperforming market occupancy in ARE’s three largest markets by 19%. That’s a massive tell about where tenant demand still exists. It’s also validation, in my view, of ARE’s strategy to pivot to these megacampuses through 2026.

Lease duration remains healthy at 7.5 years, with ~3% annual rent steps on 97% of leases and 53% of rent from investment-grade (“IG”) or large-cap public tenants. Additionally, EBITDA margins came in at an impressive 70% for Q4.

From a balance sheet perspective, ARE has +$5.3B of liquidity, an industry-leading 12+ year average debt maturity, and leverage at 5.7x net debt/EBITDA. That’s a fortress, in my eyes.

At roughly $46/share and ~170M shares outstanding, ARE’s equity market cap sits near $7.8B. When viewed against its stabilized NOI base, the stock is effectively implying a double-digit cap rate on irreplaceable megacampus life science assets. In my view, that’s a valuation that simply does not line up with the quality of the portfolio.

What Is The Outlook For ARE Stock?

The near- to medium-term outlook for ARE definitely doesn’t look great.

Founder and Chairman, Joel Marcus, didn’t sugarcoat this, either. In his remarks and in ARE’s investor presentation, ARE mentioned that 2025 marked the fifth year of a life science bear market, with oversupply across key submarkets and regulatory uncertainty adding even more pressure to an already strained biotech funding environment.

That, in turn, has translated into lower occupancy, softer same-property NOI, and near-term leasing friction that will likely carry into early 2026.

What’s more important, however, is what’s in ARE’s immediate control, and with regards to that, I believe ARE has clearly mapped a response that will enable it to overcome its challenges over the medium to long term.

ARE completed +$1.5B of dispositions in Q4 alone and laid out plans for roughly +$2.9B more in 2026, with a heavy emphasis on land and noncore assets.

The goal is straightforward. Shrink the asset base, reduce future capital needs, concentrate around Megacampuses, and preserve one of the strongest balance sheets in the REIT universe.

At the same time, leasing momentum is quietly improving, vacant space is being addressed aggressively, and nearly 900K SF of signed leases are slated to begin contributing rent in the back half of 2026.

Granted, the near term will still look messy. But the actions being taken today by ARE should benefit the REIT over the longer term.

What To Watch When ARE Reports Q1 Results?

When ARE reports its Q1, investors should be prepared for numbers that may not look pretty on the surface.

ARE has already guided to a first-quarter occupancy dip tied to +1.2M SF of lease expirations with expected downtime. Roughly 60% of those rolled in mid-January, meaning Q1 will fully reflect the vacancy before backfilling begins.

On top of that, a 171 SF lease termination in South San Francisco creates additional temporary vacancy until the new tenant commences in the second half of 2026. There’s also an expected +$6M quarterly rent reduction tied to potential tenant wind-downs.

Furthermore, same-property NOI is expected to be weakest in the first half of the year before improving later in 2026 as signed leases begin to contribute and dispositions reduce drag from noncore assets.

Investors should also watch leverage, which ARE noted could temporarily rise by 1 to 1.5 turns in Q1 due to lower EBITDA before improving as asset sales close later in the year.

In my view, the key won’t be the headline numbers. It will be leasing progress, disposition updates, and signs that the reset plan is moving forward on schedule.

Is ARE Stock A Buy, Sell, Or Hold?

I believe ARE is a ‘strong buy,’ but only for investors willing to think in years, not quarters.

In my view, the market is valuing ARE as if its real estate is permanently impaired. However, a double-digit implied cap rate on megacampus assets, a fortress balance sheet, and long-duration leases to IG tenants simply do not reconcile. I believe that disconnect is exactly where opportunity is born.

Of note is that while the Board refreshed a +$500M share repurchase authorization, ARE made it clear that no buybacks are assumed for 2026. Some would view that as a negative.

I, on the other hand, actually view that as a positive signal of discipline. Rather than using cash to prop up the share price, ARE is prioritizing dispositions, deleveraging, and reducing future capital needs. In my view, these are the right steps to strengthen the platform and unlock value the right way.

Yes, the stock could fall further after Q1. Sentiment is poor, and the numbers will likely reflect the near-term pain already outlined. But I view that as noise relative to what’s happening underneath. Asset sales, capital discipline, megacampus concentration, and signed leases that begin contributing meaningfully in 2026.

I ultimately believe this feels less like a typical REIT downturn and more like a generational dislocation in a best-in-class platform. If ARE executes on this reset, and history suggests it will, I believe today’s price could look like a rare long-term entry point.
